skills_progress.topic: Setting Up Python and Your First Script
Variables, Data Types, and Basic Operators (Project: Simple Calculator)

0.learning_objective: Set up your Python development environment and write and execute your first functional Python script by immediately applying concepts in mini-projects.

recommended_resource.title: Automate the Boring Stuff with Python (Online Edition) - Chapters 0 & 1
recommended_resource.watch: Read: Chapter 0 (Introduction & Installation) and Chapter 1 (Python Basics).
recommended_resource.reason: This resource is excellent for project-based learners. It guides you through Python installation and immediately challenges you to write practical, small scripts. This hands-on approach helps you understand your setup and see Python in action from the very beginning.

optional_resources.title: Python Tutorial for Beginners - Getting Started, Setup, and Hello World (Corey Schafer)
optional_resources.reason: A highly-rated video course that provides a clear, step-by-step visual guide for installing Python and running your first 'Hello World' script, serving as a great complement to the project-based written guide, especially if you prefer visual instructions for setup.

optional_resources.title: Learn Python 3 (Codecademy) - Introduction
optional_resources.reason: This interactive website allows you to quickly experiment with Python in your browser without any local setup. It's perfect for instantly writing and running your 'first script' and understanding basic concepts before fully committing to a local installation.

1.learning_objective: Implement variables, data types, and basic operators by building a simple calculator.

recommended_resource.title: Python Calculator Tutorial: Build a Simple Command-Line Calculator
recommended_resource.read: Focus on sections covering user input, type conversion, variables, and arithmetic operations.
recommended_resource.reason: This hands-on tutorial guides you through building a basic command-line calculator, providing practical experience with defining variables, handling user input, converting data types, and applying arithmetic operators in a real-world project.

optional_resources.title: Python Tutorial for Beginners 2: Variables, Data Types & Type Conversion
optional_resources.reason: A concise video explanation of variables, common data types (numbers, strings), and crucial type conversion concepts often needed when taking input for a calculator.

optional_resources.title: Python Arithmetic Operators
optional_resources.reason: A quick reference guide to understand the various arithmetic operators in Python, their syntax, and how they are used for calculations.

optional_resources.title: Codecademy: Learn Python 3 - Numbers & Math
optional_resources.reason: Practice using numbers and arithmetic operations in an interactive environment to solidify your understanding of basic operators and immediate feedback on your code.

0.title: Personalized Python Greetings
0.description: Implement a Python function named "create_personalized_greeting" that takes two arguments: "name" (a string) and "favorite_number" (an integer). The function should return a personalized greeting string. The greeting should follow this exact format: "Hello, [name]! Your favorite number is [favorite_number]. Did you know [favorite_number] * 2 is [result_of_multiplication]?". Ensure you use string formatting (e.g., f-strings) to construct the message efficiently.
tests: Hello, Alice! Your favorite number is 7. Did you know 7 * 2 is 14?
Hello, Bob! Your favorite number is 10. Did you know 10 * 2 is 20?
Hello, Charlie! Your favorite number is 0. Did you know 0 * 2 is 0?
Hello, Diana! Your favorite number is 150. Did you know 150 * 2 is 300?

1.title: Build a Simple Calculator Function
1.description:
Create a Python function named `simple_calculator` that takes three arguments: two numbers (`num1`, `num2`) and a string representing an arithmetic operator (`operator`). The function should perform the specified operation ('+', '-', '*', '/') on the two numbers and return the result.

For an intermediate level, your function must include error handling for division. If the operator is '/' and `num2` is 0, the function should return the string 'Error: Division by zero!'. The input numbers can be integers or floats, and the result should reflect standard floating-point division when applicable.
